coredata.dc:title: Competitor induced dissipation of carbon quantum dot based hierarchical vesicular self-assembly: A theranostic nanoplatform towards hypercholesterolemia
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Sarkar, Saheli
Mandal, Subhro
Pramanik, Animesh
coredata.dc:description:
Hypothesis Supramolecular self-assembly derived from amphiphilic molecules is one of the prime interests with the motivation to develop new building blocks to create different task-specific self-assemblies. Considering the emergent applicability of these self-aggregates across the globe, it would be necessary to develop an alternate technique for the manufacture of self-aggregates employing novel building blocks.
Experiment With this aim, we synthesized a palmitoyl moiety functionalized carbon quantum dot (FCQD). Interestingly, the synthesized FCQD was found to form a stable amphiphilic inclusion complex (βCD-FCQD) with the ‘host’ β-cyclodextrin (βCD). This amphiphilic βCD-FCQD complex was utilized as a building block to form a hierarchical vesicular self-aggregate (βCD-FCQD vesicle).
Findings This βCD-FCQD vesicle was successfully employed to detect cholesterol. Moreover, cholesterol lowering hydrophilic drug rosuvastatin loaded βCD-FCQD vesicle was found to be potential in regulation of cholesterol. This work is anticipated to encourage the construction of drug loaded self-assembly based formulation to achieve a way out towards graded combined treatment for cholesterol related disorder like hypercholesterolemia.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Dissipation of vesicle
Hierarchical self-assembly
Hypercholesterolemia
Inclusion complex
Statin drug
Theranostic nanoplatform

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formation of inclusion complex between βCD and FCQD
Investigation of self-assembling behavior and stability of βCD-FCQD inclusion complex
Microscopic characterization of the self-assembly formed by βCD-FCQD complex

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structure of surface functionalized carbon quantum dot (FCQD).
a) Fluorescence spectra of FCQD and βCD-FCQD in water at concentration 500 μg/mL (λ ex = 320 nm); b) UV-vis spectra of βCD and self-assembled βCD-FCQD complex.

a) UV titration of βCD at varying concentration of FCQD. Concentration of βCD was fixed at 100 μg/mL and concentration of FCQD was varied over the range of 10–50 μg/mL; b) Benesi-Hildebrand plot for determination of binding constant (KB) between βCD and FCQD. Here A0 is the absorbance of βCD in the absence of FCQD, A is the absorbance of βCD recorded in the presence of added FCQD and [C] is the concentration of added FCQD.
a) TEM (negatively stained); b) FESEM images of self-assembled βCD-FCQD prepared in water.
a) Solvent dependent 1H-NMR spectra of βCD-FCQD; b) Solvent dependent UV-vis spectra of ANS doped βCD-FCQD.

a) Fluorescence response of βCD-FCQD complex in water upon adding cholesterol over a concentration range 0–100 μM, b) Correlation between fluorescence intensity and concentration of cholesterol, c) Two linear range of fluorescence response of βCD-FCQD complex with varying concentration of cholesterol.
a) Emission spectra of a) Rhodamine B (RhB); b) Rosuvastatin (Rosuva) entrapped within βCD-FCQD vesicle, after treating with 100 μM cholesterol and free cargo.
Percentage (%) release of Rosuvastatin a) with varying concentration of cholesterol (chol); b) with varying time.
Schematic representation of the formation as well as cholesterol induced dissipation of βCD and functionalized CQD based vesicle.

head.ce:title: Competitor induced dissipation of carbon quantum dot based hierarchical vesicular self-assembly: A theranostic nanoplatform towards hypercholesterolemia

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Calcutta, 92, A.P.C. Road, Kolkata, 700009, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Calcutta, 92, A.P.C. Road, Kolkata, 700009, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supramolecular self-assembly derived from amphiphilic molecules is one of the prime interests with the motivation to develop new building blocks to create different task-specific self-assemblies. Considering the emergent applicability of these self-aggregates across the globe, it would be necessary to develop an alternate technique for the manufacture of self-aggregates employing novel building blocks.

With this aim, we synthesized a palmitoyl moiety functionalized carbon quantum dot (FCQD). Interestingly, the synthesized FCQD was found to form a stable amphiphilic inclusion complex (βCD-FCQD) with the ‘host’ β-cyclodextrin (βCD). This amphiphilic βCD-FCQD complex was utilized as a building block to form a hierarchical vesicular self-aggregate (βCD-FCQD vesicle).

This βCD-FCQD vesicle was successfully employed to detect cholesterol. Moreover, cholesterol lowering hydrophilic drug rosuvastatin loaded βCD-FCQD vesicle was found to be potential in regulation of cholesterol. This work is anticipated to encourage the construction of drug loaded self-assembly based formulation to achieve a way out towards graded combined treatment for cholesterol related disorder like hypercholesterolemia.

β-cyclodextrin (βCD) and fluorescent surface functionalized carbon quantum dot (FCQD) based vesicular assembly (βCD-FCQD vesicle) was developed. Cholesterol lowering drug rosuvastatin was loaded into the aqueous core of the vesicle. Through dissipation of this drug loaded vesicle an efficient method for cholesterol detection as well as regulation was demonstrated.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite significant progress in supramolecular chemistry, design and development of building blocks to create various manifestations of self-assemblies with task specific applications remains a challenging task. During the past decades, formation of self-assemblies such as micelles, vesicles, reverse micelles, gels derived from small amphiphilic molecules has been investigated at length [1–7]. As a supplement to the already established conventional supramolecular self-assemblies, hierarchical self-assemblies have become a focal point of interest within the pharmaceutical industry due to their distinct properties, including spatially controlled drug loading, responsiveness to external stimuli, and efficacy in biomolecular sensing, and so on [8–12]. A widely used host molecule cyclodextrin (CD) along with conventional guest molecules such as biopolymers, nucleic acids etc. are used as elementary molecular units to form building block amphiphiles which undergo further self-aggregation to build hierarchical self-assembly [13–17]. In recent years, with significant progress in material science, alternative guest molecules such as non-metallic nanoparticles including carbon nanotubes and graphene have also been incorporated, extending the range of CD-based hierarchical self-assembly development [18–20]. However, recently developed fluorescent carbon quantum dot (CQD) may have better suitability as a guest molecule against the ‘host’ cyclodextrin owing to its small nanoscale size (<10 nm) as well as the hydrophobicity of the carbon core. Furthermore, CQD is rapidly exploited in biomolecular detection due to its tunable photoemission property by simple variation of surface functionalization [21–28]. Previously, formation of self-assemblies derived from functionalized CQDs has also been reported [29–33]. Hence, rational as well as simultaneous utilization of CD and functionalized CQD may form an alternative building block to develop a hierarchical self-assembly. However, an interesting feature of supramolecular CD chemistry is the opportunity to replace a guest in a given CD-guest complex with a competitive guest that has a higher affinity towards CD [34]. Such a feature can also be utilized to disassemble CD-based hierarchical architectures on-demand.

By exploiting CD-based hierarchical self-assembly and CQD's tunable photoemission phenomena, we can detect as well as regulate biologically relevant specie like cholesterol to prevent heart diseases. Elevated cholesterol levels (hypercholesterolemia) are associated with life-threatening conditions like coronary heart diseases, cerebral thrombosis, and atherosclerosis [35–37]. Statins, widely prescribed medicines, reduce adverse cardiac events only by 30% [38], but high doses may cause musculoskeletal and liver toxicity [39]. Unfortunately, high cholesterol-induced heart diseases often remain asymptomatic until they become fatal. Thus, there is a high demand for a molecular system that can detect and regulate cholesterol levels to treat hypercholesterolemia with minimal side effects. While different molecular systems have been developed either for the detection of cholesterol or for the prevention of hypercholesterolemia [40–42], a single innovative system that can achieve an integration of diagnosis and treatment for hypercholesterolemia, is really scare.

Herein, we synthesized a surface functionalized CQD (FCQD, Fig. 1 ) which includes a hydrophobic carbon core and palmitoyl moiety with a lysine as well as p-phenylenediamine spacer as surface modifier. The synthesized FCQD was found to form a stable amphiphilic inclusion complex (βCD-FCQD) with the ‘host’ β-cyclodextrin (βCD). Above critical aggregation concentration (CAC), βCD-FCQD self-assembled to form a hierarchical vesicular self-aggregate by maintaining proper hydrophobic-lipophilic balance (HLB). Successively, βCD-FCQD vesicle was utilized in cholesterol detection and regulation.

Amphiphilic building block comprises of polar head and hydrophobic tail is known to form diverse manifestations of self-aggregates in different medium primarily depending on the hydrophilic-lipophilic balance (HLB) of the amphiphile. At this point, surface modified CQD might be a better choice as a building block for self-assemblies owing to its ease of surface functionalization. With the aim to synthesize a surface modified CQD, at first the precursor carbon quantum dot (PCQD; Scheme S1, Supplementary material) was prepared using citric acid. Initially, FCQD was prepared by coupling PCQD with palmitoyl moiety using lysine and p-phenylenediamine as linkers, (Fig. 1; Scheme S2, Supplementary material). We have chosen palmitoyl moiety as hydrophobic backbone because of its ease of availability as well as its established role in self-assembly. The surface functionality of the synthesized FCQD was characterized by 1H-NMR, which is given in the supplementary material.

The synthesized FCQD was readily soluble in water. At first, FCQD was characterized by 1H NMR and FTIR spectroscopy. The 1H NMR data of FCQD was provided in supplementary material. The FTIR spectrum of FCQD showed two characteristic peaks at 1700 cm−1 and 3307 cm−1 due to presence of amide (-CONH2) and amine (-NH2) functionalities, respectively indicating successful surface functionalization of PCQD (Fig. S1a, Supplementary material). The synthesized FCQD was further characterized using TEM image, which showed discrete spherical particles having size less than 10 nm (Fig. S1b, Supplementary material). Moreover, the aqueous solution of FCQD showed blue emission having emission maxima at 418 nm upon excitation at 320 nm (Fig. 2 a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of inclusion complex between βCD and FCQD
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of FCQD to form a stable inclusion complex with βCD was further investigated by ITC experiment (Fig. S2, Supplementary material). The sigmoidal binding curve observed in Fig. S2 confirmed successful formation of βCD-FCQD inclusion complex. From ITC experiment, the KB value was found to be 7.12 × 102 M−1 (obtained from the instrumental analysis), which further ensures the stability of βCD-FCQD inclusion complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of self-assembling behavior and stability of βCD-FCQD inclusion complex
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, the structural feature of βCD-FCQD complex showed an amphiphile-like arrangement where βCD acting as the hydrophilic “head group” and the palmitoyl long chain of the guest molecule FCQD acting as hydrophobic “tail”. This triggered us to study the self-assembling behavior of amphiphilic βCD-FCQD complex. Primarily, βCD-FCQD complex formed a translucent solution in pure water at 0.5 mg/mL concentration (Fig. S3a, Supplementary material). This unique phenomenon indicates the possible self-aggregation of the amphiphilic βCD-FCQD complex. Prior to the self-aggregation study, the stability of the translucent solution of βCD-FCQD complex was checked. The stability index of this solution was found to be 98% (Fig. S3a, Supplementary material).

Surface tension method was used to calculate the critical aggregation concentration (CAC) of the amphiphilic βCD-FCQD complex in water. As the concentration of a surface-active substance is increased, the surface tension of a liquid steadily decreases. After attaining the CAC value, any further addition of surfactant molecules will only increase the number of aggregations. Here, a wide concentration (10–2000 μg/mL) range was used to determine the surface tension of the βCD-FCQD complex Fig. S3b, Supplementary material). The break point indicates that βCD-FCQD complex has self-aggregation property. The CAC value of the βCD-FCQD complex, where the amphiphile begins to form the self-aggregated structure, was found to be 241 μg/mL. Microscopic and spectroscopic experiments were conducted on this self-assembled system to determine its morphology as well as aggregation mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic characterization of the self-assembly formed by βCD-FCQD complex
ce:section.ce:para: The negatively stained TEM image exhibited spherical self-aggregated structure having an average diameter of 60–90 nm indicating the possible construction of a vesicular morphology in water by the self-assembly of βCD-FCQD complex (Fig. 4 a). The development of vesicular self-aggregates by βCD-FCQD complex was further substantiated by FESEM studies (Fig. 4b). FESEM images also exhibited the vesicular aggregates of βCD-FCQD complex in water having diameter of 80–100 nm in good agreement with TEM study. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) study was performed to further ensure the formation of vesicular structures by the self-assembly of βCD-FCQD complex. The AFM image of βCD-FCQD complex in water showed spherical vesicles having diameter in the range of 80–100 nm (Fig. S4a, Supplementary material), which is in concurrence with the TEM and FESEM images.

DLS experiment was carried out to obtain the mean hydrodynamic diameter and the size distribution of βCD-FCQD vesicles. The size distribution (expressed in number percentage) profile for the amphiphile is shown in Fig. S4b. It is clearly observed that the size distribution for βCD-FCQD vesicles was in the range 80–100 nm (Fig. S4b, Supplementary material) having hydrodynamic diameter ∼90 nm, which is in concurrence with the preceding microscopic observations.

Steady state fluorescence anisotropy measurement is often used to investigate the microenvironment of an aggregated structure. DPH is a well known fluorescence probe, which is used to determine the fluorescence anisotropy (r) for various aggregated structures including micelles, vesicles, bilayers, etc. [29,47] Herein, the r-value of self-assembled βCD-FCQD complex was determined using DPH (Table S1, Supplementary material). Over a concentration range from 0.5 to 2 mg/mL, the r value of βCD-FCQD vesicles increased from 0.07 to 0.16. DPH is a well-known membrane fluidity probe that can readily fit inside the hydrophobic area of the vesicle because of its rigid, rod-like structure [47]. The higher the restriction in the movement of DPH in the hydrophobic domain, the larger the anisotropy value. DPH shows stronger fluorescence anisotropy in vesicles compared to micellar aggregates. Herein, the observed r values of DPH are also significantly higher compared to that of micellar aggregates formed from sodium dodecyl sulfate (r = 0.054). This fact further confirms the formation of vesicular assembly by βCD-FCQD complex.

Next, we carried out solvent dependent 1H-NMR study for βCD-FCQD complex to investigate the non-covalent interactions between self-associating molecules (Fig. 5 a). In DMSO-d6, the aromatic protons of p-phenylenediamine of βCD-FCQD complex showed a sharp peak at δ = 7.99. In DMSO-d6, βCD-FCQD complex is in a molecularly dissolved state without any self-aggregation. On addition of D2O within DMSO-d6 (1:1 v/v, DMSO-d6−D2O), the aromatic protons of p-phenylenediamine of βCD-FCQD complex got upfield shifted from δ = 7.99 to 7.78 ppm, accompanied by the decrease in NMR signal intensity. Notably when βCD-FCQD complex was taken in D2O, the NMR signals of aromatic protons almost diminished along with further upfield shifting in the δ value to 7.61 ppm. This observation delineates the involvement of hydrophobic as well as intermolecular π-π interaction between the aromatic moieties of the amphiphilic βCD-FCQD complexes owing to the self-aggregation.

At this point, we were curious to understand the molecular level aggregation pattern for βCD-FCQD complex. The aggregation pattern of βCD-FCQD complexes was investigated from solvent dependent UV-vis spectra of 8-anilinonaphthalene-1-sulfonic acid (ANS, 1 × 10−5 M) doped within the mentioned βCD-based amphiphiles (0.5 mg/mL) (Fig. 5b). ANS is prone to localize itself at the hydrophobic domain of the self-aggregates. UV-vis spectra of ANS doped within βCD-FCQD complexes were recorded in varying solvents from DMSO (non-self-assembled state) to water (self-assembled state). UV absorption maxima of ANS blue shifted from 380 nm (in DMSO) to 376 nm–373 nm in 1:1 (v/v) DMSO-water and water, respectively (Fig. 5b). As reported earlier, blue-shifted absorption maxima correspond to self-organization of molecules through parallel plane-to-plane stacking termed H-type of aggregation [2]. Thus, the observed blue-shifted absorption maxima clearly designate the H-type aggregation for the self-assemblies of βCD-FCQD complexes.

Furthermore, the emission intensity of FCQD in self-assembled state of βCD-FCQD complexes was found to decrease (Fig. 2a) possibly due to the self-quenching of intrinsic fluorescence of CQDs upon self-aggregation of the amphiphiles.

Ensuring the successful formation of the vesicle by the self-assembled amphiphilic βCD-FCQD complexes and exploiting tunable photoemission property of FCQDs, we have planned to investigate the fluorescence response of βCD-FCQD vesicles towards cholesterol. Therefore, varying concentration (0–100 μM) of cholesterol was incubated with βCD-FCQD vesicular solution (0.5 mg/mL). Upon increase in the cholesterol concentration, the fluorescence signal of FCQD at 418 nm gradually increased (Fig. 6 a). At 100 μM cholesterol, almost 80% fluorescence intensity was restored with respect to the fluorescence intensity of native FCQD (0.5 mg/mL). This fluorescence enhancement of FCQD in the presence of cholesterol could be attributed to the rupture of the βCD-FCQD vesicular aggregate due to destruction of the βCD-FCQD complex. βCD and cholesterol form a very stable inclusion complex having binding constant 1.7 × 104 M−1

or 4.4 × 10−2 mL/μg [40], which is much higher compared to the binding constant of βCD-FCQD complex. Hence, FCQD is displaced from the cavity of βCD in presence of competitor molecule, cholesterol. Consequently, the βCD-FCQD based vesicular aggregate is dissipated due to the change in HLB. The detection limit was as low as 1 μM. The fluorescence intensity versus concentration of cholesterol was fitted in a curve over a cholesterol concentration range of 0–100 μM (Fig. 6b). Importantly, a plot of the fluorescence response (defined as (F – F0)/F0, where F0 is the emission intensity of FCQD within self-assembled state of βCD-FCQD and F is the emission intensity of displaced FCQD) versus the concentration of cholesterol showed two linear relationships in two different concentration range (1–20 μM and 20–100 μM) (Fig. 6c). However, the cholesterol induced disassembly of the βCD-FCQD based vesicular aggregate was further investigated by TEM study. Interestingly, both FESEM and TEM images of βCD-FCQD vesicular solution (0.5 mg/mL) in presence of 100 μM cholesterol showed spherical particles having size less than 10 nm (Fig. S5, Supplementary material), which is comparable to the size of FCQDs (Fig. S1, Supplementary material). This fact further establishes the cholesterol induced dissipation of vesicular aggregates. As the binding constant of βCD and cholesterol is very high, cholesterol displaces FCQD to form more stable βCD-cholesterol inclusion complex leaving behind free FCQDs (Scheme 1 ).

This fact also implies that βCD-FCQD complex can have high potential to scavenge cholesterol from a cholesterol rich microenvironment leading to decrease the cholesterol level. However, in presence of 0.5 mg/mL βCD-FCQD vesicular solution, we found no alteration of fluorescence intensity of FCQD by increasing cholesterol concentration beyond 100 μM. Hence, βCD-FCQD vesicular solution having concentration 0.5 mg/mL can scavenge 100 μM cholesterol. Hence, cholesterol level can be regulated by the dissipation of βCD-FCQD hierarchical self-assembly.

Salts, amino acids, carbohydrates, lipids, and other bio components found in human blood serum can interfere with cholesterol detection and hamper the selectivity of the βCD-FCQD nanosensor. Therefore, we studied fluorescence response of βCD-FCQD vesicle in presence of a wide range of species which can interfere with the detection process of cholesterol in the blood serum. It is quite evident from Fig. S6 that salts like NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, phosphate, sulfate, carbohydrates like glucose and amino acids like glycine, tyrosine showed negligible Interference even at the concentration of 1 mM. The selctivity of βCD-FCQD nanosensor was also screened through other biologically relevant molecules such as ascorbic acid, vitamin-E, dithiothreitol (DTT), BSA protein and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) which also could not affect the fluorescence signal of βCD-FCQD nanosensor in notable extent (Fig. S6, Supplementary material). Thus, the newly developed βCD-FCQD nanosensor can selectively detect cholesterol over the other biologically relevant molecules.

To this end, the interior aqueous core of vesicle is known to be proficient for entrapping a large number of cargos. Hence, cargo entrapment efficacy of the newly developed βCD-FCQD vesicle was investigated using a water-soluble fluorescent dye rhodamine B (RhB), which is known to accommodate itself in the inner aqueous compartment of the vesicle. RhB was successfully encapsulated within βCD-FCQD vesicle and unentrapped dye was separated by dialyzation. This dye entrapped vesicles were examined under a fluorescence microscope which showed red emitting spheres indicating the successful entrapment of RhB within the aqueous core of vesicles (Fig. S7, Supplementary material). Encapsulation of the dye was further ensured from the comparative fluorescence study of the free RhB and entrapped RhB in vesicles at 578 nm (λ ex = 546 nm). It was found that the fluorescence intensity of the RhB entrapped within vesicle was notably reduced compared to that of free dye (Fig. 7 a) possibly due to its self-quenching property under confined conditions.

The loading percentage of the encapsulated dye was also determined by rupturing the vesicles in presence of cholesterol (100 μM). The RhB loading was found to be ∼83% for βCD-FCQD vesicle, as calculated from the standard calibration curve (absorbance Vs concentration) of free RhB. The release of the entrapped dye from βCD-FCQD vesicles was also investigated by fluorescence study. The emission intensity of entrapped RhB in βCD-FCQD vesicles was found to be low. However, upon treating with cholesterol, the emission intensity notably increased (Fig. 7a). This observation confirms the release of dye molecules from the inner aqueous core of the vesicle to the bulk solvent during the dissipation of vesicular aggregates in the presence of cholesterol.

Ensuring superior cargo entrapment efficacy, cholesterol-lowering drug rosuvastatin was loaded within the βCD-FCQD vesicle. Entrapment of rosuvastatin within the βCD-FCQD vesicle was confirmed from the UV-visible study, where a characteristic absorbance peak was obtained at λ max = 241 nm, indicating the presence of the drug within the vesicles (Fig. S8a, Supplementary material). Loading efficiency of βCD-FCQD vesicles was found to be 85%, as calculated from the calibration curve (Fig. S8b, Supplementary material). Moreover, the release study of encapsulated drug from the βCD-FCQD vesicles was also investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy. The emission intensity of entrapped rosuvastatin in βCD-FCQD vesicles was found to be lower compared to the emission intensity of free rosuvastatin having the same concentration (Fig. 7b). The emission intensity of rosuvastatin was revived to some extent upon addition of cholesterol. This observation supports the release of statin drug from the inner aqueous core of the vesicle to the bulk solvent due to the disintegration of vesicular aggregates in the presence of cholesterol. However, statins are class of drugs which reduce the production of cholesterol by blocking some specific enzyme. Hence, after dissipation of statin loaded βCD-FCQD vesicles the body cholesterol level can be reduced synergistically in two ways such as by scavenging existing cholesterol through βCD and by preventing cholesterol production due to delivery of statin drugs.

Ensuring successful loading of drug within βCD-FCQD vesicle, we intended to investigate the release profile of the drug from the vesicle. In this regard, rosuvastatin was loaded within βCD-FCQD vesicle. As βCD-FCQD vesicle undergoes dissipation upon interaction with cholesterol, we carried out concentration dependent drug release kinetics with varying concentration of cholesterol. Previously, it was found that the fluorescence intensity of the released drug got increased compared to the entrapped drug (Fig. 7b). Thus, the % release of drug was calculated from the relative change in the fluorescence intensities at the emission maximum. Interestingly, at 80 μM concentration of cholesterol up to 83% drug got released instantaneously from the drug loaded βCD-FCQD vesicle (Fig. 8 a). This can be attributed to the burst release of the drug molecules due to the significant change in permeability of the bilayer membrane of the vesicle upon interaction with cholesterol. Moreover, a drug release profile with varying time was also investigated. Interestingly it was found that 90% of drug got released within 8 min in presence of 50 μM cholesterol (Fig. 8b) indicating rapid initial burst release of the drug molecules. Therefore, this drug loaded βCD-FCQD vesicle has a potentiality in regulation of cholesterol.

To this end, the prospective application of rosuvastatin loaded βCD-FCQD vesicle in treatment of hypercholesterolemia primarily depends on its potentiality in detection as well as regulation of cholesterol. At this point, βCD-FCQD vesicle showed high fluorescence responsiveness in presence of cholesterol. Hence, it may be utilized as an efficient diagnostic agent for early detection of hypercholesterolemia. Moreover, rosuvastatin loaded βCD-FCQD vesicle has a potentiality in regulation of cholesterol due to its synergistic effect by inhibiting cholesterol production through rosuvastatin as well as accelerating cholesterol scavenge through βCD. Thus, βCD-FCQD vesicle may also act as a therapeutic agent in treatment of hypercholesterolemia.

To utilize this βCD-FCQD vesicle as a theranostic agent, two fundamental requirements are needed to be satisfied i) sufficient stability of the vesicular assembly in biological media, ii) higher cytocompatibility towards mammalian cells. The stability of βCD-FCQD vesicle was investigated in FBS (0–75%) containing DMEM media. βCD-FCQD vesicular solution was added within FBS containing DMEM media and stability of the solution was studied for 48 h. Similarly, the long-term stability of βCD-FCQD vesicle was studied in 10% FBS containing DMEM for 10 days. The final concentration of βCD-FCQD vesicle in each case was maintained to 0.5 mg/mL. However, the suspension stability index of βCD-FCQD vesicle in media with increasing FBS content and also for 10 days was found to be >90% (Fig. S9, Supplementary material). Visual observations also showed that βCD-FCQD vesicle was sufficiently stable in cell culture media with increasing FBS concentration up to 75% (Fig. S9, Supplementary material). Ensuring the high stability of the prepared vesicle in biological media, the cytocompatiblity of βCD-FCQD vesicle was examined against mammalian cells by MTT assay. Varying concentrations of βCD-FCQD vesicular solutions (250–1000 μg/mL) were added to CHO cells. Interestingly after 24 h incubation, it was found that the cell viability of βCD-FCQD vesicle was around 90% (Fig. S10, Supplementary material). Hence, βCD-FCQD vesicle fulfilled both fundamental requirements for its utilization in theranostic applications.

In summary, we developed a novel amphiphilic βCD-FCQD complex exploiting simple host-guest chemistry between β-cyclodextrin and functionalized CQD. This inclusion complex is further self-assembled to form a vesicular aggregate in aqueous medium. Interestingly, this βCD-FCQD vesicle acts as a fluorescence sensor for the detection of cholesterol with a detection limit of 1 μM. Moreover, the self-assembly of βCD-FCQD complex was readily dissipated in presence of cholesterol due to the displacement of CQD from the newly developed complex by the competitive binder (cholesterol) with relatively higher affinity. Thus, βCD-FCQD vesicle also helps to reduce cholesterol level by scavenging cholesterol from a cholesterol-rich microenvironment. Furthermore, a cholesterol-lowering drug rosuvastatin was loaded within βCD-FCQD vesicle. Upon cholesterol induced dissipation of the vesicle, the statin drug got released, which can further reduce the cholesterol level by preventing its production. Hence, the simultaneous detection as well as reduction of cholesterol level utilizing cholesterol triggered dissipation of β-cyclodextrin and CQD based hierarchical self-assembly followed by the release of cholesterol-lowering drug, may emerge a new paradigm towards the treatment of hypercholesterolemia with a better clinical outlook.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: S. S. thanks D. S. Kothari unit of University Grants Commission (UGC-DSKPDF), India for offering Dr. D. S. Kothari postdoctoral fellowship. S. M. thanks University Grants Commission (UGC), New Delhi, India for offering Senior Research Fellowship (SRF). Authors thank Centre for Research in Nanoscience & Nanotechnology (CRNN-CU) and DST-FIST for instrumental facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data to this article:

Materials and methods, Table for steady-state fluorescence anisotropy of βCD-FCQD, Synthetic schemes of PCQD and FCQD, FTIR spectrum and TEM image of FCQD, ITC graph the binding interaction of FCQD to βCD, Stability index and CAC graph of βCD-FCQD complex, AFM image and DLS plot of self-assembled βCD-FCQD complex, FESEM and TEM images after dissipation of self-assembled βCD-FCQD in presence of cholesterol, Fluorescence response of βCD-FCQD vesicle in presence of different biomolecules, Fluorescence microscopic images of rhodamine b encapsulated βCD-FCQD vesicle, UV-vis spectrum of encapsulated rosuvastatin in βCD-FCQD vesicle, calibration plot of rosuvastatin, media stability of βCD-FCQD vesicle, cell viability of βCD-FCQD vesicle.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jciso.2023.100094.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spontaneous Formation of a vesicular assembly by a trimesic acid based triple tailed amphiphile
